sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 72-year-old woman, reports she is doing well but struggles with not drinking alcohol, having previously mentioned a significant alcohol use disorder.

She was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 milligrams by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 milligrams by mouth once daily at her last visit, but has been taking only half the dose of lisinopril due to side effects.
She has been taking her blood pressure medications, but has been drinking four to five times a week, with the last drink being on Monday.
She reports her blood pressure is higher in the evenings, but improves when she sits for a while.
She is taking her medicine in the morning instead of in the evening.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of mild hypercholesterolemia and significant alcohol use disorder.
Review of systems: The patient reports no fever, cough, shortness of breath, or sore throat.
She has had the coronavirus vaccine on February 9th.
Current medications:
Lisinopril 10 milligrams by mouth once daily for hypertension.
Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 milligrams by mouth once daily for hypertension.
Objective:
Vital signs:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient is alert, pleasant, in no acute distress.
The skin is moist and good.

The patient is not diabetic, with no tremor, no CVA tenderness, chest clear to percussion and auscultation, unlabored breathing, cardiac rhythm regular, no murmur, no gallop, JVP flat, with a head at 90 degrees.
The patient does not appear anxious, agitated, or depressed.
Test Results: CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an alcohol use disorder and is a candidate for treatment with naltrexone to help with cessation.
Problem list: Alcohol use disorder; Hypertension confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Naltrexone, to be administered via injection on May 17th.
I discussed the potential side effects and risks with the patient.
I will see her back in the week of June 14th, which is likely around the time of her second Naltrexone injection.
I recommend upgrading her influenza vaccination and Shingrix, which she previously deferred.
Follow-up:
The patient will return on May 17th for the first Naltrexone injection and on June 14th for a follow-up appointment.
